findings.rationale: The upstream property has no definition and its label (avsSystem) does not provide enough evidence to confirm it matches the AVCP system concept.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream property has no definition and its label 'avsSystem' (AVS) does not reliably correspond to the AVCP-system concept, so a match cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to a coding system, but ours is specifically the AVCP system, a particular instance of a general coding system.
findings.qaRationale: Our term identifies which CPR Annex V assessment-and-verification-of-constancy-of-performance system applies to a product's essential characteristics, whereas schema.org codingSystem names a medical/classification coding scheme like ICD-10; these are unrelated concepts, not a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: The upstream term is a generic class definition, whereas our term specifies a particular fire‑reaction classification.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific domain class for EN 13501-1 fire-reaction classification, while schema.org/Class is merely the generic metaclass for any type, so they denote unrelated concepts, not a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: Reaction-to-fire Class is a specific classification per EN 13501-1, whereas Criterion is a general condition for evaluation.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a concrete fire-performance classification system (EN 13501-1 classes A1–F), not an abstract evaluation condition; a domain classification class and a generic conformity Criterion class are different concepts, so the proposed NARROW is rejected.

findings.rationale: Reaction-to-fire Class is a specific classification per EN 13501-1, whereas Criterion is a generic condition for evaluation.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a concrete fire-performance classification class per EN 13501-1, while UNTP Criterion is a generic conformity-assessment evaluation condition; these are different concepts, not a genus-species pair, so reject.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a performance statement, but ours specifically links to a structured Declaration of Performance, whereas the candidate is a generic performance claim.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking a product to a performance assertion, but ours points to a whole regulated CPR document that bundles many declared characteristics whereas untp:performanceClaim references a single structured conformity claim, so the relationship is overlapping rather than a clean subsumption either way.

findings.rationale: Both properties associate a product with a structured value, but ours is specifically for a Declaration of Performance, whereas typeOfGood is a generic link to any structured value.
findings.qaRationale: schema:typeOfGood points from a structured value to the product it concerns (inverse direction and generic), whereas ours points from a construction product to its Declaration of Performance document, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties link a construction product to its Declaration of Performance, matching in purpose and semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking a construction product to its Declaration of Performance and the abbreviation 'DoP' is unambiguous in this domain, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm identical range/cardinality, so interchangeability cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the same European Assessment Document linked to a Declaration of Performance.
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name strongly suggests the same European Assessment Document concept, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm interchangeable domain/range and semantics, so EXACT cannot be safely asserted.

findings.rationale: The property refers to any characteristic name, whereas the GS1 term specifies only additive or genetic modification names.
findings.qaRationale: Our term names an essential characteristic of a construction product, while gs1:additiveName names a food additive or genetic modification; these are unrelated concepts despite the superficial 'name' overlap, so no SKOS mapping holds.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the entity that issued a European Technical Assessment, with identical scope and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: Both name the Technical Assessment Body that issued a European Technical Assessment, but ours is typed as a property whose range is oec:OperatorInformation while the upstream term has no definition or stated range to confirm interchangeability, so a weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the entity that issues an assessment or review, but the upstream term is specific to reviews rather than technical assessments.
findings.qaRationale: schema:reviewBody is the textual content (a Text) of a review, whereas eucpr:technicalAssessmentBody identifies the organization that issued a technical assessment, so they denote unrelated concepts despite the shared word 'body'.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the declared thermal conductivity of a product, with identical units and domain context.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties named thermal conductivity and plausibly the same physical quantity, but the upstream term has no definition, domain, or unit specification to confirm interchangeability, so an EXACT match is unwarranted.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies thermal conductivity, a particular measurement, whereas the upstream term is a generic measurement value.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:value is the bare numeric scalar slot inside a measurement (range: float), whereas eucpr:thermalConductivity is a product attribute whose range is a whole gs1:QuantitativeValue object, so they are different kinds of property, not a narrower/broader pair.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the reaction‑to‑fire classification of a construction product, matching in scope and intent.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties about a construction product's reaction-to-fire, but the upstream term lacks a definition and its name ('reactionToFire' vs our 'reactionToFireClass') suggests it may carry general fire-reaction behavior rather than strictly the EN 13501-1 class, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: The upstream property refers to any additional product classification, whereas the OUR term specifies a particular reaction‑to‑fire class.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's additionalProductClassification concerns product-category taxonomy codes, whereas our term is a fire-safety performance grade under EN 13501-1, which is a different concept rather than a narrower kind of product classification.

findings.rationale: The property refers to a specific EN 13501‑1 fire‑reaction classification, whereas the candidate is a generic additional product classification code; our term is more specific.
findings.qaRationale: GS1's term is a generic product-taxonomy classification code (categorizing what the product is), whereas our term is a specific EN 13501-1 fire-performance grade, so they are not the same concept despite the shared word 'classification'.

findings.rationale: EssentialCharacteristic is a specific type of Criterion, representing a declared property value; thus it is narrower than the general evaluation condition.
findings.qaRationale: An essential characteristic is a declared property name+value+unit pairing per CPR Annex III, not an evaluation condition; semantically it is closer to a declared claim/property than to cv:Criterion, so if forced onto Criterion our term sits at best loosely adjacent and is not narrower than it.

findings.rationale: The GS1 class covers any quantitative value, while the OUR class specifies a single essential characteristic with name and standard reference.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:QuantitativeValue is a generic measurement-value datatype (point or interval with a unit), whereas eucpr:EssentialCharacteristic is a CPR Annex III declared-performance concept bundling name, value, unit, and harmonised-standard reference, so they are different concepts rather than a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: EssentialCharacteristic is a specific declared property value, whereas Criterion is a general evaluation condition.
findings.qaRationale: An essential characteristic is a declared product property (name+value+unit), whereas a UNTP Criterion is an evaluation condition/requirement to assess conformity against, so they are different kinds of concept rather than a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: EssentialCharacteristic is a specific declared property value, whereas Requirement is a general condition that must be proven.
findings.qaRationale: EssentialCharacteristic is a declared product property (name+value+unit) per CPR Annex III, not a condition-to-be-proven-by-evidence; the closer SEMICeu match would be a Characteristic/property-value concept, so Requirement is a different concept and should be rejected.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the URI of the harmonised technical specification under which an essential characteristic is declared.
findings.qaRationale: Both properties reference a harmonised standard, but the upstream term lacks any definition or stated domain, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same class of construction products governed by the DPP and Declaration‑of‑Performance framework.
findings.qaRationale: Both are construction-product classes, but ours is explicitly scoped to EU Regulation 2024/3110 as a DPP/DoP carrier while the undefined DPP Keystone class is a generic construction-product concept, making ours a regulation-specific subset rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric measurement qualified by a unit code, but the candidate lacks the specific domain of an essential characteristic.
findings.qaRationale: Our term restricts a quantitative measurement value to the domain of an essential characteristic (range gs1:QuantitativeValue), whereas gs1:value is the generic unqualified measurement-value property, so ours is the narrower specialization of the upstream concept — making upstream broader and ours NARROW... correcting: from OUR perspective ours is NARROW relative to the broader gs1:value.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a quantitative value for an essential characteristic, whereas schema.org’s value is a generic property applicable to any QuantitativeValue.
findings.qaRationale: Both are property-typed value carriers, but ours is range-restricted to gs1:QuantitativeValue and domain-bound to an essential characteristic while schema.org:value is a generic, untyped value slot spanning a different (schema.org) QuantitativeValue lineage, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same concept of a structured declaration of performance for construction products.
findings.qaRationale: Both are classes for a construction-product Declaration of Performance, but the upstream term lacks any definition to confirm it carries the same structured CPR Article 12 fields, so interchangeability cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Declaration of Performance is a specific type of performance declaration defined by CPR Article 12, whereas the UNTP Performance class is a generic, unspecified performance concept.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a CPR Article 12 legal compliance document (a class of regulatory declaration), whereas UNTP Performance is a generic performance/metric concept, not a declaration document, so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Construction Product Type represents a top‑level product family, whereas ConstructionProduct in DPP Keystone denotes an individual construction product.
findings.qaRationale: Our term denotes a product-family/category concept (CPR Annex III families used to classify products), which is a different concept from the upstream class denoting a construction product itself, so it is a metalevel mismatch rather than a broader-than relation.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the notified body involved in a declaration, sharing identical domain and range semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Both name the notified body involved in conformity assessment, but the upstream term has no definition and our property is scoped specifically to a CPR DeclarationOfPerformance domain with an oec:OperatorInformation range, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and EXACT is too strong.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name and refer to the issuance date of a declaration, indicating identical scope.
findings.qaRationale: Upstream dppk:dateOfIssue has no definition and is presumably a generic document/passport issuance date, whereas ours is constrained to the issuance of a Declaration of Performance, making our term a narrower specialization rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the date an item is issued, though the specific domain (Declaration vs. ticket) differs.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote the issuance date of a formal credential/document, but schema.org dateIssued is scoped to tickets/certifications while ours is specific to a Declaration of Performance, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the original issuance date of a document, but one refers to a declaration and the other to a certification.
findings.qaRationale: Issuance date of a self-declared Declaration of Performance (a manufacturer's regulatory document) is not the same concept as the original issuance date of a third-party certification, so these properties attach to distinct document types and are not interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties record the first issuance date of a document, but one is specific to a Declaration of Performance while the other applies broadly to any published creative work or certification.
findings.qaRationale: A regulation-specific date-typed property on a Declaration of Performance is not the same concept as schema.org's general publication/broadcast date on a CreativeWork, despite overlapping issuance semantics.

findings.rationale: Both properties refer to the same concept of linking test or validation reports that support a declaration.
findings.qaRationale: Identical name and property type in a shared DPP context strongly suggest the same concept, but the upstream term carries no definition, so EXACT interchangeability cannot be confirmed and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the declared compressive strength of a construction product, using the same quantitative value representation.
findings.qaRationale: Both are construction-product compressive-strength properties with matching labels, but the upstream term lacks any definition or stated value representation, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and EXACT is unwarranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the unique reference number identifying a Declaration of Performance.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream property shares the same name and likely refers to a declaration reference number, but with no definition given and our term being CPR-specific (Declaration of Performance, Article 12), interchangeability cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the unique identifier for a Declaration of Performance.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is the CPR-specific Declaration of Performance reference number with a typed DeclarationOfPerformance domain, whereas the undefined upstream dopIdentifier lacks a definition and domain constraint, so identity cannot be confirmed and only an approximate overlap is warranted.
